It is only in the last three fiscal years that we’ve seen a meaningful pivot, with ROIC finally scaling toward 8.5%. This improvement suggests that the "efficiency" era led by the C-suite is finally taking hold, though the challenge remains whether they can maintain this trajectory while their core growth engine shifts gears.

A closer look at the capital structure reveals a massive expansion in equity, ballooning from USD 15 billion in 2019 to USD 58 billion by 2022. This wasn't organic growth; it was a byproduct of the "growth-at-all-costs" era, primarily driven by shares issued for major acquisitions.

The aggressive onboarding of Tableau and Slack—funded through a heavy mix of stock and cash—has left a lasting mark on the balance sheet. In fact, goodwill now accounts for over 50% of total assets, a clear indicator of the high premium paid for past growth. This equity bloat was further exacerbated by consistent layers of stock-based compensation.

This structural inefficiency is precisely what drew the attention of activist investors, who successfully pivoted management toward operational discipline and aggressive capital return. The numbers speak for themselves: nearly USD 20 billion in share repurchases over the last three fiscal years (USD 4 billion in 2023, USD 7.6 billion in 2024, and USD 7.8 billion in 2025). While this is a massive effort to shrink the denominator, my concern remains the long-term value proposition.

I evaluate long-term compounding through a simple lens: Long-term compounding = f(Growth, ROIC, P/E). For a company to be a true compounder, it requires stable, preferably double-digit revenue growth and a high, consistent ROIC. The P/E multiple typically follows as a byproduct of these two drivers.

In Salesforce’s case, we are seeing a disconnect. Revenue growth has cooled to 9%, and even with management’s guided 11% CAGR, the "high-growth" narrative is under pressure. While the aggressive buyback program is a necessary tool to artificially boost ROIC, the improvement is gradual. If the market expects 9-11% growth paired with a mid-teen ROIC, there is little fundamental reason for the P/E multiple to expand materially. The real question for investors is whether Salesforce can find a way to consistently surpass that 11% ceiling; without that acceleration, the stock lacks the necessary catalyst for a major rerating.

The Integration Costs For A Client: A Material Risk For Salesforce

A critical factor that most investors overlook is the structural friction embedded in Salesforce’s growth history. While competitors like ServiceNow were built on a single, modular codebase from day one, Salesforce has expanded through a decade of aggressive, disparate acquisitions. Integrating Slack (messaging), Tableau (analytics), MuleSoft (middleware), and most recently Informatica (data management) into a cohesive ecosystem is not a simple "plug-and-play" exercise.

Each of these acquisitions brought its own database architecture and metadata language. Because the core "Clouds" (Sales, Service, Marketing) were not designed to share a native heart, the company is now forced to build expensive, complex bridges just to make AI functional. The very necessity of the Informatica acquisition in 2025 serves as an indictment of this fragmentation; if the platform were truly unified, Salesforce wouldn't need to spend billions on a tool specifically designed to clean, move, and "master" messy data.

To make Agentforce 360 work, enterprises are often forced to physically ingest or duplicate data into Data Cloud. This creates a "Data Duplication Tax"—clients essentially pay for storage twice while maintaining complex pipelines to keep that data in sync. If these pipelines fail, the AI’s reasoning becomes unreliable, grounded in stale or "hallucinated" data records.

In contrast, ServiceNow’s "Single Platform" architecture operates on a modular "switch" logic. When a client needs a new capability, the module is simply toggled on within the existing environment. There is no need for expensive integrations or data replication because the data never left the platform. This architectural elegance leads to a faster ROI for the client and higher margins for the provider.

Having benchmarked ServiceNow’s efficiency first, the contrast in Salesforce's execution becomes clear. Even a "win" in the Salesforce ecosystem highlights this friction. During the Q4 FY2026 call, management celebrated SharkNinja standing up an agent in eight weeks—a timeline that, in a truly unified, modular environment, would be considered a lag rather than a milestone. This "Integration Tax" is what a major Salesforce client alluded to in the recent Q4 call:

Mark Adam Barrocas

SharkNinja, Inc.

Look, Marc, I mean, we launched 25 products a year, and we're really innovating at speed. And we need customer service solutions that move just as fast. I mean most companies treat service as a cost center. For us, Marc, it's really about lifetime value of a consumer. I mean we view service as a growth engine for the business. And it's not just about servicing problems, it's about building lifetime value.

We set up with you and your team, a guided shopping agent in 8 weeks right before the holiday season. I was nervous about it as I went to my team and I said, we're putting this in place in October. There's generally kind of a cutoff in our business where after October 1, you don't really do anything. And we launched this in 8 weeks, and it brought tremendous value to the consumer. I mean, it helped them with researching and buying and troubleshooting really all in one seamless conversation. So it was a great success for us this holiday season.

The friction in Salesforce’s architecture is most visible when looking at time-to-value. During the Q4 FY2026 call, management highlighted a client implementation that took eight weeks to deploy a guided shopping agent—a pre-built tool within the Agentforce (formerly Einstein) ecosystem. While Salesforce frames this as a success, it looks sluggish when benchmarked against the competition. In 2025 and early 2026, ServiceNow documented multiple retail and utility clients deploying Now Assist agents for customer service in just 14 to 21 days.

This leads to the primary reason I am wary of Salesforce's long-term dominance: ServiceNow is effectively "invading" the front office from the back.

In the current market, enterprises are no longer satisfied with a CRM that simply records a customer complaint; they demand immediate resolution. Because ServiceNow already controls the operational "organs" of the company—IT, HR, and supply chain—their Customer Service Management (CSM) can trigger a back-end fix, such as a refund or a technician dispatch, almost instantly. Salesforce, by contrast, must build a bridge via Data Cloud or MuleSoft just to reach those same internal systems.

The financial divergence is already showing up in the numbers. Salesforce is fighting to maintain a 9.6% YoY growth rate, while ServiceNow continues to scale at north of 20%. My view is that surpassing the guided 11% CAGR will require a Herculean effort from Salesforce. Until they can prove that their "Integration Tax" isn't a permanent drag on client ROI, it is difficult to justify a higher multiple for the stock.

Valuation

According to SA Quant ratings, the stock appears attractive, as several valuation multiples suggest Salesforce is trading at a discount relative to its primary peers.

To gain more granular control over the underlying projections, let's move toward a valuation model based on the following specific assumptions:

  • Outstanding shares: 956 million

  • FCF Margins: 24% (representing the 10-year historical average)

  • Holding Period: I am assuming the stock is held through the year 2030.

  • Revenue Growth: 11% CAGR (consistent with management’s long-term guidance)

  • Terminal P/FCF (2030): 18x. While the P/FCF fluctuated between 23x and 26x over the last four years, it dipped toward 14x in FY2026. I am modeling 18x on the assumption that Salesforce rebuilds investor confidence as it executes on its 2030 targets.

  • Discount rate: 9%

Based on management’s recent guidance, I have modeled revenue projections through 2030, reaching an estimated USD 63 billion. This trajectory implies a compound annual growth rate of 11%. By applying a forecasted 24% FCF margin to these top-line figures, we arrive at an estimated 2030 free cash flow of USD 15.1 billion.

To determine the per-share value, I divided that USD 15.1 billion by the 956 million shares currently outstanding, resulting in an FCF per share of USD 15.80 for 2030. Applying an 18x multiple to this figure yields a 2030 target price of USD 284.80. To find the current fair value, I discounted this target back to 2026.

The intrinsic value is calculated as follows:

Intrinsic value = 284.8/(1+discounted rate)^4.

This calculation produces an approximate intrinsic value of USD 198 per share, suggesting that the current stock price offers no meaningful margin of safety. From a strategic standpoint, Salesforce may face significant headwinds in delivering that 11% annual growth. Its platform architecture often necessitates higher integration costs and lacks the operational efficiency seen in competitors like ServiceNow.

Risk

The primary risk to my thesis is the possibility that Salesforce successfully reduces integration costs for its clients in the coming years. By combining a more aggressive sales posture and higher customer satisfaction levels with robust share buybacks to sustain ROIC, the company could achieve performance that surpasses current projections. This scenario could drive annual revenue growth north of 11% and push ROIC toward 15%, likely resulting in an expansion of the stock’s P/E multiple.

Nevertheless, I maintain that the odds are not in Salesforce's favor, as competition has reached unprecedented levels. The firm must battle players like ServiceNow, which already possesses a streamlined architecture capable of scaling at a faster clip. While I am not concerned about the immediate AI threat—given that Salesforce has effectively fortified its ecosystem through a strategic partnership with Anthropic—the rapid advancement of other industry peers remains the chief concern. Ultimately, the consequences of a mismanaged growth strategy could lead to slower-than-expected expansion, triggering a downward revision in long-term expectations and hindering the stock's appreciation potential.

Takeaways

Salesforce’s strategic alliance with Anthropic suggests that the sensationalist "SaaSapocalypse" narrative is likely the least of the company's structural worries. The real pressure comes from peers like ServiceNow, whose superior scaling strategy is evidenced by consistent revenue growth exceeding 20% in recent years.

To remain competitive, Salesforce must prove that its hybrid model of aggressive acquisitions and internal R&D can deliver a superior ROI per dollar of integration cost relative to its more streamlined rivals. My previous deep dive into ServiceNow’s architecture highlighted a more efficient scaling mechanism that offers clients a clearer path to value.

Beyond horizontal competition, Salesforce faces significant friction from niche specialists like Veeva Systems in the healthcare sector—a topic I’ve covered extensively. Ultimately, Salesforce will need to exert considerable effort to outperform its 11% CAGR guidance if it hopes to restore long-term market confidence.

data.title: Goldman Sachs Inflation Protected Securities Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Overview

In the US, we expect a continued expansion of global economic growth in 2026, with most major economies contributing to above-trend growth. Activity should stay robust, underpinned by strong AI-related capital expenditure, still-easy financial conditions, and a positive fiscal impulse from the "One Big Beautiful Bill". We expect the Fed to cut rates further in 2026, but above-trend growth argues for only limited additional easing. That said, the appointment of a dovish new Fed Chair could point to somewhat more room for cuts, depending on the extent to which the new Chair manages to convince the rest of the Federal Open Market Committee of their policy views.

Inflation: The all items index rose 2.7 percent for the 12 months ending December, the same increase as over the 12 months ending November. The all items less food and energy index rose 2.6 percent over the last 12 months. The energy index increased 2.3 percent for the 12 months ending December. The food index increased 3.1 percent over the last year.

Growth: We expect a continued expansion of global economic growth in 2026, with most major economies contributing to above-trend growth. In the US, activity should stay robust, underpinned by strong AI-related capital expenditure, still-easy financial conditions, and a positive fiscal impulse from the "One Big Beautiful Bill". Global economies may face near-term headwinds from tariffs but remain supported over the medium term due to robust private sector fundamentals, looser and expansive monetary and fiscal policy, and accelerated AI capital expenditures. Heading into 2026, we believe growth in the US faces potential drag from trade and immigration, but risks may be mitigated by policy easing, fiscal stimulus, and prospective AI investments. Economies outside the US, such as the Euro area and Japan, are also expected to remain resilient, aided by easing monetary policy, expansive fiscal stimulus, and trade.

The Bloomberg U.S. TIPS Index returned -0.40% in December and the Intermediate TIPS (1-10yrs) Index returned -0.19%. This is compared to the Bloomberg U.S. Treasury Index, which returned -0.33% and the Intermediate Treasury Index, which returned 0.67%.

Performance and Attribution

The Fund's I-shares returned 0.16% net over the fourth quarter and outperformed the benchmark by 2bps.

The Fund's specific government and yield curve trades contributed to performance, primarily driven by our US 2y3y Steepener and US 2y5y Steepener. The US Yield Curve steepened in Q4'25, as the Fed continued its cutting cycle. Short-term yields trended downwards as long-term yields remained elevated due to inflation concerns. Additionally, elevated term premia likely driven by fiscal and institutional risks kept long-term yields elevated as well. Spreads between short-term yields and long-term yields widened. This widening between the short-end and long-end led to our steepeners outperforming.

Strategy/Outlook

Multiple factors could impact economic conditions and financial markets in 2026, making it challenging to predict a clear direction. We believe active investment management is well-suited for navigating such conditions. An investment approach that goes beyond simply following benchmarks is important heading into 2026, in our view, especially with asset valuations elevated. This may involve actively managing portfolios and tilting towards areas where we see potential value and income opportunities, such as small-cap equities and securitized credit. We believe diversification across alternative assets can enhance risk-adjusted returns.

Labor market dynamics hold the key to Federal Reserve (Fed) easing into 2026, in our view, so long as inflation remains anchored. As tariffs settle at lower (but not low) levels than initially seemed likely, we believe they still present a downside risk to growth. Global government debt has surged past $100 trillion, and the fiscal backdrop is a key concern that could trigger market volatility.

Policy: We believe the labor market holds the key to the pace and scale of Fed easing into 2026, so long as inflation remains anchored. If labor market weakness persists, driven by immigration restrictions, federal layoffs, and labor-saving AI, additional Fed rate cuts may follow, especially if tariff-induced inflation is short-lived. We expect the ECB to maintain its 2% rate, given at-target inflation and German fiscal expansion, though we do not rule out a return to rate cuts if inflation begins to moderate. In the UK, we believe easing inflation, a softening labor market, and fiscal contraction support further easing. Disinflation, Fed cuts, and signs of further dollar weakness could lead to further easing in emerging markets, with country variations. Japan remains on track towards a higher rate regime, in our view, backed by firm inflation, strong GDP growth, and potential fiscal easing. With policy rates becoming less restrictive and no tariff-induced recession, rate hikes in other economies are also possible, signaling a new phase in global monetary policy.

We expect fiscal policy to remain in focus in 2026, with a new economic package in Japan, federal spending accelerating in Germany and potential new measures to be announced in the US ahead of the Midterm elections.

Overall, our current TIPS positioning is slightly overweight vs. the benchmark (being underweight to shorter-dated TIPS and overweight to longer-dated TIPS), and we maintain long-term conviction in the asset class. In today's market, TIPS offer historically elevated real yields and inflation protected principal. This is powerful in providing investors high levels of expected return, income protection in an uncertain inflationary environment, and diversification benefits in fixed income portfolios.

Fund Performance

For periods one year or greater, performance is annualized. The returns represent past performance. Past performance does not guarantee future results. The Fund's investment return and principal value will fluctuate so that an investor's shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance quoted above. Please visit Financial Intermediaries to obtain the most recent month-end returns. Performance reflects cumulative total returns for periods of less than one year and average annual total returns for periods of greater than one year. All Fund performance data reflect the reinvestment of distributions.

Please refer to the Fund's prospectus for the most recent expenses.

Definition of Terms

Bps: basis points, or 1/100th of 1%

CAI: Goldman Sachs Asset Management's Current Activity Indicator (CAI) offer a real-time economic snapshot by analyzing over 300 high-frequency activity data points, expressed in GDP terms. These tools enable us to quickly identify economic shifts and adapt our investment strategies accordingly

Bps: basis points, or 1/100th

CPI: Consumer Price Index

PCE: Personal Consumption Expenditure Price Index

PPI: Producer Price Index

TIPS: an acronym for Treasury Inflation-Protected Security

TIPS Breakeven: calculated by subtracting the yield of an inflation-protected bond from the yield of a nominal bond

Hawkish (monetary policy): monetary policy focused on controlling inflation by either raising interest rates or keeping interest rates at elevated levels

Dovish (monetary policy): tends to suggest lower interest rates; opposite of hawkish

FOMC: Federal Open Market Committee

Source: Bloomberg, Goldman Sachs Asset Management. As of 31-Dec-2025.

Risk Considerations:

The Goldman Sachs Inflation Protected Securities Fund invests primarily in inflation protected securities (IPS) of varying maturities issued by the U.S. Treasury and other U.S. and non-U.S. Government agencies and corporations. Fixed income securities are subject to the risks associated with debt securities generally, including credit, liquidity and interest rate risk. IPS are fixed income securities whose interest and principal payments are periodically adjusted according to the rate of inflation. The market value of IPS is not guaranteed, and will fluctuate in response to changes in real interest rates. The market for IPS may be less developed or liquid, and more volatile, than certain other securities markets. If deflation were to occur, IPS would likely decline in price. Any guarantee on U.S. government securities applies only to the underlying securities of the Fund if held to maturity and not to the value of the Fund's shares. The Fund's investments are also subject to market risk, which means that the value of the securities in which it invests may go up or down in response to the prospects of individual companies, particular sectors or governments and/or general economic conditions. Derivative instruments may involve a high degree of financial risk. These risks include the risk that a small movement in the price of the underlying security or benchmark may result in a disproportionately large movement, unfavorable or favorable, in the price of the derivative instrument; the risk of default by a counterparty; and liquidity risk (i.e., the risk that an investment may not be able to be sold without a substantial drop in price, if at all). The Fund's investments in other investment companies (including ETFs) subject it to additional expenses.

Views and opinions are current as of the date of this commentary and may be subject to change, they should not be construed as investment advice. Consumer Price Index (CPI) is measured by the changes in the price level of a market basket of consumer goods and services purchased by households.

The "core" PCE price index is defined as Personal Consumption Expenditures (PCE) prices excluding food and energy prices. The core PCE price index measures the prices paid by consumers for goods and services without the volatility caused by movements in food and energy prices to reveal underlying inflation trends.

Producer Price Index (PPI) is measured by the average changes in prices received by domestic producers for their output.
